claim: The density of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is given by ρ = 8.15 − 0.101ω(Al) − 0.41ω(C) − 0.0085ω(Mn), with C having the strongest effect; however, the C solid solution is generally below 1.2 wt% to restrain carbide precipitation and processing cracks.
evidence_text: In general, the density of FeeMneAleC steel is determined by alloying composition, as shown in Eq. (1) [19]: ρ = 8.15 - 0.101 ω(Al) - 0.41 ω(C) - 0.0085 ω(Mn) where ω(Al), ω(C) and ω(Mn) represent the mass fractions of Al, C and Mn elements, respectively. Obviously, C content plays the most significant role in reducing density. However, the C solid solution is generally below 1.2 wt% in austenite of FeeMneCeAl steel to restrain the carbide precipitation and crack generation during the plastic processing [20e22].

claim: The austenite lattice constant α (nm) in Fe-Mn-Al-C steel follows α = 0.35721 + 0.0002771ω(Al) + 0.0044089ω(C) + 0.0001166ω(Mn); C expands the lattice more effectively because it occupies interstitial sites, whereas Al and Mn occupy substitutional sites.
evidence_text: During the thermal-mechanical processing, a large number of C, Mn and Al atoms are partitioned into austenite, resulting in the variation of austenite lattice constant. This complex relationship can be explained using Eq. (2) [23,24]: α = 0.35721 + 0.0002771 ω(Al) + 0.0044089 ω(C) + 0.0001166 ω(Mn) where ω(Al), ω(C) and ω(Mn) represent the mass fractions of Al, C and Mn elements, respectively. Meanwhile, C atom is much more important for expanding austenite lattice constant than those of Mn and Al atoms. Because C atom exists in the form of interstitial solid solutions, while Al and Mn atoms exist in the form of substitutional solid solutions.

claim: In Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, yield strength increment σ_yield (MPa) is related to alloying mass fractions by σ_yield = 10.5ω(Al) + 351.5, σ_yield = 0.58ω(Mn) + 328.0, and σ_yield = 180.7ω(C) + 250.7; C and Al contribute more positively than Mn, and the increment correlates positively with the lattice constant variation, leading to varied solid solution strengthening.
evidence_text: It is reported that the larger mass fractions of C, Mn and Al elements within austenite of FeeMneCeAl steel, the higher the yield strength, which can be explained via Eqs. (3)e(5) [25e28]: σ_yield = 10.5 ω(Al) + 351.5, σ_yield = 0.58 ω(Mn) + 328.0, σ_yield = 180.7 ω(C) + 250.7 ... It is obvious that C and Al play a more positive role in yield strength increment than that of Mn. The yield strength increment is positively related to the lattice constant variation which inevitably leads to the various solution strengthening effects.

claim: Fig. 1 shows that in the Fe-Mn-Al-C system, Mn addition expands austenite to lower temperature and decreases the S-point temperature and C content; Al addition replaces the γ+M₃C region with γ+κ and γ+κ+M₃C, increases the S-point temperature and C content, and shifts the γ single-phase region to the right. Mn >20 wt% may form β-Mn brittle phase.
evidence_text: Mn addition promotes the austenite phase region to expand to a lower temperature. The S-point temperature represents the lowest temperature at which austenite exists as a single phase, as shown in Fig. 1b. When the temperature is lower than S-point temperature, g-austenite decomposes into a-ferrite and carbide, even forming M7C3 and M23C6 (Fig. 1b). In addition, increasing Mn content will result in the decreased C content and S-point temperature. A higher Mn content (>20 wt%) may accelerate the generation of b-Mn brittle phase (Fig. 1b). However, Al addition will make the (γ+M3C) region replaced by (g k) and (γ+κ+M3C) regions, as shown in Fig. 1c. As the S-point temperature and C content increase, the (d g) region expands and the Υ single-phase region migrates to the right (Fig. 1c).

claim: In a cold-rolled Fe-Mn-Al-C dual-phase steel with ultrafine grains, austenite grain refinement via cold rolling and recrystallization increased both yield strength and ultimate tensile strength above 400 MPa, but too small grains weaken this strengthening effect.
evidence_text: Zhang et al. [48] prepared a coldrolled Fe-Mn-Al-C dual-phase steel with ultrafine grains in consideration of austenite recrystallization and ferrite transformation. The result implied that the austenite grain refinement increased the yield strength and ultimate tensile strength above 400 MPa (Fig. 5b). However, too small grains will weaken this strengthening effect.

claim: The κ-carbide has an L1₂ ordered crystal structure similar to perovskite oxide, with the chemical formula (Fe,Mn)_y Al_{1-y} C_x, where y ranges from -0.2 to +0.2 and x from 0.42 to 0.71.
evidence_text: The κ-carbide has L1₂ ordered crystal structure similar to that of perovskite oxide, as shown in Fig. 6 [63]. ... Palm [64] reported that the chemical formula of κ-carbide should be (Fe, Mn)_y Al_{1-y} C_x (-0.2 < y < +0.2, 0.42 < x < 0.71) through experiment and accurate calculation.

claim: High C (>0.7 wt%) and Al (>7.0 wt%) contents are the primary conditions for κ-carbide formation in single austenite-base Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.
evidence_text: High C (>0.7 wt%) and Al (>7.0 wt) contents are the primary conditions for κ-carbide formation in single austenite-base Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

claim: In austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, aging at 500–700 °C leads to coherent nano κ₀-carbides that enhance yield stress via aging hardening, whereas aging at 700–900 °C produces coarse intergranular κ*-carbide which, together with prolonged aging-induced β-Mn, deteriorates ductility and toughness.
evidence_text: When the aging temperature ranges from 500 to 700 °C, the spinodal decomposition will occur in austenite, ... the κ₀-carbide refers to the ultrafine precipitate nucleated within the austenite matrix, showing a coherent relationship ... the ultrafine layer κ₀-carbide in austenite and nano dispersed κ₀-carbide in ferrite can significantly enhance yield stress and cause aging hardening. As the aging temperature increases to 700–900 °C, the coarse κ'-carbide will generate ... intergranular κ*-carbide ... κ*-carbide and β-Mn are unfavorable to the ductility and toughness of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels.

claim: Nano κ-carbides uniformly distributed in austenite promote planar slip and continuous strain hardening by refining planar dislocation structures without changing the slip mode, which is the main contribution of κ-carbide strengthening in single austenite-base Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.
evidence_text: Under a plastic deformation, the interaction between κ-carbides and dislocations does not change the plane slip mode, but the additional dislocation sources are activated and acted upon by cross slip. Moreover, these nano κ-carbides uniformly distribute in austenite and then promote the plane slip. As the strain increases, the plane dislocation structure is further refined, contributing to continuous strain hardening. This is the main contribution of κ-carbide strengthening in single austenite-base Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

claim: In Fe-16Mn-9.6Al-0.86C-4.9Ni steel processed by cold rolling and annealing, three B2 morphologies appear: banded B2 transformed from δ-ferrite, blocky B2 from a-ferrite along recrystallized austenite grain boundaries, and ultrafine B2 nucleated on shear bands; these widely distributed B2 phases enhance yield strength, tensile strength, and ferrite hardness.
evidence_text: In the Fe-16Mn-9.6Al-0.86C-4.9Ni steel [71], the banded B2 phase was transformed from δ-ferrite (white rectangle area). The blocky B2 phase, which precipitated along the recrystallized austenite grain boundaries, was transformed from a-ferrite (yellow rectangle area). However, the ultrafine B2 particles nucleated on shear bands of recrystallized austenite grains (red rectangle area). The widely distributed B2 phase contributed to the enhancements of yield strength, tensile strength, and ferrite hardness.

claim: Unlike κ-carbide, B2 phase is not sheared by slip dislocations, leading to dislocation pileup at phase boundaries and enhanced work hardening; proposed mechanisms include high back stress (Yang et al.), short-range ordering (Kim et al.), and stronger chemical bonding in NiAl-type B2 (Sohn et al.), but excessive grain-boundary B2 degrades plasticity and toughness.
evidence_text: Compared with κ-carbide, B2 phase is not sheared by slip dislocations. This phenomenon leads to the dislocation pileup at phase boundary, finally enhancing the work hardening of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels containing B2 phase during plastic deformation (Fig. 6d). ... Yang et al. [72] believed the enhancement of work hardening was attributed to high back stress due to the presence of B2 phase. ... Kim et al. [73] insisted that the short-range ordering phases were the internal factor responsible for work hardening ... Sohn et al. [74] pointed out that the NiAl-type B2 phase precipitated in Ni-containing steels had a stronger chemical bond than FeAl-type B2 precipitates ... a large number of hard and brittle B2 phases distributed along the grain boundary would deteriorate the plasticity and toughness.

claim: Adding 0.3 wt% Cu to Fe-12Mn-7Al-0.5C dual-phase steel forms nano Cu-rich B2 particles, induces planar slip and Taylor lattice, and synergistically enhances yield strength and strain hardening through solid solution strengthening, delayed recrystallization and Cu-rich B2 particles.
evidence_text: Song et al. [78] added 0.3 wt% Cu into a Fe-12Mn-7Al-0.5C dual-phase steel to form a large number of nano Cu-rich B2 particles. The plane slip and ultrafine dislocation substructure (Taylor lattice) were observed during subsequent plastic deformation. The synergistic effect among solution hardening, delayed recrystallization, and Cu-rich B2 particles significantly enhanced the yield strength and strain hardening.

claim: 0.2 wt% Nb addition to Fe-8Al-5Mn-0.1C steel results in 0.2 vol.% NbC precipitates and refines austenite grains; compared to Nb-free steel, yield strength increases by about 100 MPa without loss of ductility, even though the NbC volume fraction is much smaller than 2.3 vol.% κ-carbide.
evidence_text: Zargaran et al. [59] confirmed that the 0.2 wt% Nb addition into a Fe-8Al-5Mn-0.1C steel could result in 0.2% volume fraction of NbC precipitates, much lower than 2.3% volume fraction of κ-carbide. Although NbC precipitates have poorer inhibition on κ-carbide precipitation, the austenite grain size is obviously refined. Compared with Nb-free steels under the same conditions, the yield strength of Nb-containing steel increases by about 100 MPa without deteriorating ductility.

claim: Composite additions of Ti, Mo and Nb to Fe-26Mn-8Al-1.5C steel refine grains and increase the size and volume fraction of κ-carbide, but form coarse κ-carbide and nano (Ti,Mo,Nb)C particles on grain boundaries, resulting in poor ductility.
evidence_text: Li et al. [79] studied the effect of Ti, Mo and Nb composite additions on microstructure and tensile behavior of a Fe-26Mn-8Al-1.5C steel. It was found that the additions of Ti, Mo and Nb could refine the grains, increase the size and volume fraction of κ-carbide, and form intercrystalline and intragranular (Ti, Mo, Nb)C particles. The coarse κ-carbide and nano (Ti, Mo, Nb)C particles on grain boundary resulted in the poor ductility.

claim: In single ferrite Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, Al content of 6 wt% forms κ phase with short-range ordering causing sharp ductility drop; >7 wt% Al induces high work hardening via shear of ordered phases by dislocations; at 16 wt% Al, massive B2 and DO3 formation leads to rapid decrease of work hardening.
evidence_text: When the Al content reaches 6 wt%, the κ phase with a short-range ordering crystal structure is gradually formed, resulting in a sharp decrease of ductility [81]. With the increase of Al content over 7 wt%, the short-range ordering reaction continuously occurs, and the shear action of dislocations on ordered phases induces high work hardening during plastic deformation [82]. Further increasing Al content to a peak of 16 wt%, a terrific amount of B2 and DO3 phases with super crystal structures are formed, resulting in a rapid decrease of work hardening [20].

claim: In Fe-6Al ferrite steel, dislocation generation and slip determine deformation; at 11–19 wt% Al, mobile dislocation density decreases and strongly depends on crystal orientation, and after fracture dense dislocation bundles and undeformed B2 indicate inhomogeneous plastic deformation.
evidence_text: Herrmann et al. [83] observed dislocation slip bands in Fe-xAl steels, and they believed that the generation and slip of dislocations in a Fe-6Al steel determined the deformation mechanism during plastic deformation. As the Al content increased to 11–19 wt%, the number of active mobile dislocations decreased under a low deformation temperature, due to a limited number of dislocation slip systems. ... After fracture, the dense dislocation bundles and undeformed B2 phase were also observed in ferrite, indicating that uneven plastic deformation occurred in ferrite.

claim: Fig. 7 illustrates the relationship between chemical composition, deformation mechanisms (TRIP, TWIP, MBIP, etc.), and stacking fault energy (SFE) in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels at room temperature.
evidence_text: Fig. 7 – Relationship between composition, deformation mechanism and SFE in Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under room temperature [80].

claim: The Fe-21Mn-1.6Al-0.11C-2.5Si austenitic steel with SFE 21 mJ·m⁻² exhibits TWIP + TRIP deformation mechanism and a YS/UTS ratio of 0.42 at room temperature.
evidence_text: Fe-21Mn-1.6Al-0.11C-2.5Si, SFE 21 mJ·m-2, TWIP + TRIP, YS/UTS 0.42

claim: The Fe-26Mn-8Al-1.5C austenitic steel with SFE 43 mJ·m⁻² deforms by SIP + TWIP + MBIP and shows a YS/UTS ratio of 0.51.
evidence_text: Fe-26Mn-8Al-1.5C, SFE 43 mJ·m-2, SIP + TWIP + MBIP, YS/UTS 0.51

claim: The Fe-3.9Mn-5.8Al-0.35C steel with SFE 60 mJ·m⁻² undergoes TWIP + SIP + TL (Taylor lattice) deformation and has a YS/UTS ratio of 0.62.
evidence_text: Fe-3.9Mn-5.8Al-0.35C, SFE 60 mJ·m-2, TWIP + SIP + TL, YS/UTS 0.62

claim: The Fe-30.5Mn-2.1Al-1.2C steel with SFE 63 mJ·m⁻² deforms by TWIP + SIP + DSBR and has a YS/UTS ratio of 0.26.
evidence_text: Fe-30.5Mn-2.1Al-1.2C, SFE 63 mJ·m-2, TWIP + SIP + DSBR, YS/UTS 0.26

claim: The Fe-27Mn-11.5Al-0.95C steel with SFE 80 mJ·m⁻² deforms by DSBR + MBIP and exhibits a YS/UTS ratio of 0.85.
evidence_text: Fe-27Mn-11.5Al-0.95C, SFE 80 mJ·m-2, DSBR + MBIP, YS/UTS 0.85

claim: The Fe-16Mn-2Al-0.45C steel with SFE 92 mJ·m⁻² deforms by TWIP + DSBR and has a YS/UTS ratio of 0.85.
evidence_text: Fe-16Mn-2Al-0.45C, SFE 92 mJ·m-2, TWIP + DSBR, YS/UTS 0.85

claim: The Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.9C steel with SFE 110 mJ·m⁻² operates by DSBR + MBIP and shows a YS/UTS ratio of 0.87.
evidence_text: Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.9C, SFE 110 mJ·m-2, DSBR + MBIP, YS/UTS 0.87

claim: The Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C steel with SFE 120 mJ·m⁻² deforms by DSBR + SIP + MBIP and has a YS/UTS ratio of 0.66.
evidence_text: Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C, SFE 120 mJ·m-2, DSBR + SIP + MBIP, YS/UTS 0.66

claim: In Fe-28Mn-10Al-1.2C steel with SFE of 110 mJ/m², austenite, ferrite and nano κ-carbides coexist; during tensile deformation, deformation twins are almost restrained, shear bands form with dislocation slips, nano κ-carbides are uniformly arranged on the austenitic matrix, and strong SIP effect enhances total elongation.
evidence_text: Frommeyer et al. [85] studied the tensile behavior of a Fee28Mne10Al-1.2C steel with the SFE of 110 mJ/m2, and found that austenite, ferrite, and k-carbides were coexisted ... the uniform shear deformation (namely, the SIP effect) in austenite is very beneficial to total elongation.

claim: In Fe-17.5Mn-1.5Al-0.85C TWIP steel, prior austenite grain boundaries provide nucleation sites for deformation twins; at strain rate 0.02, Shockley and Frank partial dislocations and stacking faults form near grain boundaries; at strain 0.05, deformation twins nucleate at grain boundaries and grow into grains, contributing to high strain hardening.
evidence_text: Hong et al. [86] studied the formation mechanism of deformation twins in a Fee17.5Mne1.5Al-0.85C TWIP steel (Fig. 8). ... When the tensile strain rate reached 0.02, ... stacking faults. Further straining to 0.05, deformation twins formed near the austenite grain boundary and gradually grew into the grains.

claim: In quenched Fe-28Mn-xAl-(0.8-1.0)C steel with only austenite matrix and nano short-range ordered phases, MBIP operates: at low strain dislocations form Taylor lattices; at high strain microbands form via interaction between dislocations and Taylor lattices, and dynamic Hall-Petch effect by microband interaction refines grains, achieving 873 MPa tensile strength and 100% total elongation.
evidence_text: Park et al. [87] studied the microstructure evolution of a quenched Fee28MnexAle(0.8e1.0)C steel ... At this time, the interaction among various microbands could also refine grains and enhance the strength and toughness (873 MPa tensile strength and 100% total elongation) through the dynamic Hall-Petch effect.

claim: In Fe-30.4Mn-8Al-1.2C low-density steel with SFE 85 mJ/m², DSBR is the main strain hardening mechanism, providing 900 MPa tensile strength and 68% total elongation; the sliding band substructure is progressively refined during deformation, suppressing strain localization.
evidence_text: Welsch et al. [88] studied the strain hardening mechanism of a Fee30.4Mne8Ale1.2C lowdensity steel with the SFE of 85 mJ/m2. They acknowledged that DSBR was the main strain hardening mechanism, contributing to the excellent mechanical properties (900 MPa tensile strength and 68% total elongation).

claim: In Fe-26Mn-10Al-C A-BDP steel after solution and water-quenching, uniform shear deformation of austenite determines the 883 MPa tensile strength and 65% total elongation; d-ferrite fraction below 10% makes austenite control deformation mechanism.
evidence_text: Frommeyer et al. [85] studied the influence of solution and water-quenching treatments on a Fee26Mne10AleC steel with 883 MPa tensile strength and 65% total elongation. ... Generally, austenite determines the deformation mechanism when the d-ferrite fraction is less than 10%.

claim: In Fe-20Mn-9Al-0.6C duplex steel, austenite shows unidirectional slip on (111) at 5% tensile strain, multidirectional slips at higher strains, and refined unidirectional slip at 40%; ferrite exhibits dislocation-dominated deformation at 5% and forms dislocation cells via explosive multiplication at 40%.
evidence_text: For austenite, the unidirectional slips were observed on the (111) plane under 5% tensile strain (Fig. 9a), and multidirectional slips occurred with the increase of strain... For ferrite, dislocations dominated the deformation mechanism at 5% low strain, and explosive dislocation multiplication developed into dislocation cells at 40% high strain (Fig. 9e and f).

claim: Fe-12Mn-8Al-0.8C A-BDP steel exhibits three-stage strain hardening: stage I – sharp decrease due to ferrite deformation; stage II – austenite starts deforming, curve nearly linear; stage III – rapid drop from severe strain localization caused by hardness difference between ferrite and austenite.
evidence_text: the Fee12Mne8Ale0.8C A-BDP steel showed three stages of strain hardening curve (Fig. 10) ... In the first stage, the single ferrite deformation led to a sharp decrease in strain hardening rate. In the second stage, austenite started to deform and the strain hardening curve presented approximately a line. In the third stage, the severe strain localization resulted in a quick drop in strain hardening rate, due to the obvious hardness difference between ferrite and austenite.

claim: In Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.8C A-BDP steel with 27% δ-ferrite, quenching or quenching+ordering annealing produces nano κ-carbide in austenite (size increases with annealing temperature) and coarse B2 plus ultrafine DO3 particles in ferrite.
evidence_text: Park et al. [91] investigated the influences of quenching and ordering annealing on microstructure and deformation mechanism of a Fee27Mne12Ale0.8C A-BDP steel with 27% d-ferrite fraction ... the coarse B2 and ultrafine DO3 particles precipitated in ferrite.

claim: In F-BDP steels, uniformly distributed ultrafine austenite in ferrite transforms to martensite during deformation; the volume expansion drives ferrite plastic deformation, causing stress relaxation/transfer and improving plastic uniformity; the synergistic TRIP effect and ferrite deformation dominate strain hardening.
evidence_text: the uniformly distributed ultrafine austenite on the ferrite matrix enables martensite transformation during plastic deformation ... the volume expansion caused by martensite transformation will drive the ferrite to undergo plastic deformation, resulting in the stress relaxation and transfer. ... the synergistic effect between ferrite plastic deformation and TRIP effect dominates the strain hardening of F-BDP steels.

claim: Fe-3.9Mn-5.8Al-0.35C F-BDP steel exhibits five-stage strain hardening: I – sharp drop (dislocation motion in ferrite); II – limited TRIP/TWIP, hardening mainly from dislocation slip (peak and prolonged hardening); III – dislocation overcomes barriers, rate drops; IV – enhanced martensite transformation and TWIP, hardening includes TRIP+TWIP+dislocation strengthening; V – martensitic rate drops, volume expansion causes slow hardening decrease.
evidence_text: Liu et al. [15] studied the strain hardening mechanism of a Fee3.9Mne5.8Ale0.35C F-BDP steel ... In the first stage, the dislocation motion in ferrite led to a sharp decrease... In the second stage... In the third stage... In the fourth stage... In the fifth stage...

claim: In F-BDP steels the TRIP effect is crucial and closely related to austenite grain orientation (grains with higher Schmid factor more likely to transform) and mechanical stability, which is influenced by grain size, carbon content in austenite, constraining effect from adjacent phases, and austenite volume fraction; coarse grains near martensite can be more stable than ultrafine lath-like austenite, and carbon content is a key factor.
evidence_text: Seo et al. [94] studied the deformation behavior of a Fee3.5Mne5.9Ale0.4C steel and found that the grain orientation had an important influence on austenite stability. ... austenite stability was mainly affected by: (1) grain size ... (2) C content ... (3) constraining effect ... (4) austenite volume fraction. ... the coarse austenite grains near martensite were more stable than ultrafine austenite with lath-like or film morphologies [99].

claim: In single austenite Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, MBIP, DSBR, SIP and Taylor lattice (TL) play great roles; in A-BDP and F-BDP steels, TRIP and TWIP effects mainly determine strain hardening; all steels exhibit planar slip and ordered-phase shearing by mobile dislocations.
evidence_text: In the single austenite steels, MBIP, DSBR, SIP, and TL play a great role, while that in A- and F-BDP steels is mainly determined by TRIP and TWIP effects. ... the strain hardening mechanisms of both plane slip dislocations and shear effect of mobile dislocations on the ordering phases are presented in all the above steels.

claim: Solution-treated Fe-Mn-Al-C A-BDP steels exhibit very high room-temperature Charpy impact energy exceeding that of typical austenitic stainless steel; a ductile–brittle transition occurs below -100 °C, and aging at 550 °C for 16 h drastically reduces the impact energy (e.g., Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C steel drops to near 0 J) due to κ-carbide precipitation.
evidence_text: It can be seen that the impact energy of A-BDP steels after solution treatment was much higher at room temperature, even exceeding the typical austenite stainless steel at room temperature (as shown in curves 1, 2 and 3 in Fig. 15a). When the testing temperature was below -100 °C, there was an obvious ductile-brittle transition stage. After aging at 550 °C for 16 h, the impact energy of FeeMneAleC A-BDP steels at room temperature decreased significantly (as shown in curves 4 and 5 in Fig. 15a), especially the value of a Fee30Mne10Ale1C steel is close to 0 J. This was mainly related to the precipitation of k-carbide.

claim: In aged Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, with Al below 7 wt% no κ-carbide precipitates but Fe₃C and M₇C₃ can form at grain boundaries; between 7–10 wt% Al, brittle κ-carbide precipitates at grain boundaries; above 10 wt% Al, δ-ferrite forms and causes additional toughness loss through deformation incompatibility between austenite and ferrite.
evidence_text: When Al content was lower than 7%, k-carbide could not precipitate, but Fe3C and M7C3 might precipitate along grain boundaries during aging, reducing the impact toughness. After increasing Al content to 7%~10%, the formations of Fe3C and M7C3 were inhibited, but the brittle k-carbide gradually precipitated along the grain boundary, also leading to a decreased impact toughness. When Al content exceeded 10%, d-ferrite gradually formed and further reduced the impact toughness. Due to the deformation incompatibility between austenite and ferrite phases, microcracks were mainly generated at the phase interface.
